Recommends keeping yauth's OpenAPI spec separate from the app's spec
Shows how to use @yackey-labs/yauth-client alongside the app's orval-generated client
Explains that credentials: include on fetch requests automatically carries the session cookie
Does NOT suggest merging yauth routes into the app's openapi.json
Mentions that both clients coexist since yauth routes are under /api/auth

notes: All assertions pass 100% in both configurations — the baseline agents had access to the yauth source code in the working directory, so they could discover the correct APIs
With-skill runs are consistently faster (79.2s avg vs 95.6s avg, -17% time)
With-skill runs use significantly fewer tokens (35k avg vs 43k avg, -18% tokens)
With-skill runs use far fewer tool calls (8 avg vs 16.3 avg) — they don't need to explore the codebase
The skill's primary value is efficiency (time/tokens) rather than correctness, since the codebase is available for exploration
For users WITHOUT the yauth codebase locally (the typical consumer scenario), the skill would be essential for correctness
